title: HTTP Semantics, Roles, Representations, and Layered Evidence · Browser and Node Client Systems · TypeScript Build Room
summary: An accessibility product team integrating a staging browser API must build a first protocol model from narrated fixtures and explain each policy boundary for http semantics, roles, representations, and layered evidence, producing protocol evidence map. Planned artifact: Build a complete first version of a TypeScript 7 standards-based Fetch protocol evidence map while every earlier competency remains active. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal deb…
objectives: Distinguish client, server, user agent, origin, intermediary, gateway, proxy, and cache roles per exchange. Demonstrate the result through TypeScript 7 strict types plus WHATWG Fetch Request, Response, Headers, URL, streams, and AbortSignal contracts.

Separate a resource from the selected representation and its metadata, content, and current validity. Demonstrate the result through TypeScript 7 strict types plus WHATWG Fetch Request, Response, Headers, URL, streams, and AbortSignal contracts.

Trace request construction, routing, transport, intermediary handling, response interpretation, and cleanup as distinct stages. Demonstrate the result through TypeScript 7 strict types plus WHATWG Fetch Request, Response, Headers, URL, streams, and AbortSignal contracts.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

steps.title: Fetch and TypeScript Commit to the mission model · Workshop Client Server Roles
steps.instruction:
workshop case — A workshop accessible staging dashboard SDK request T-4 crosses a resolver, TLS connection, gateway, cache, and origin before a changed payload is rejected. Separate every role, representation, state holder, and failure layer before editing code. Competency probe: Distinguish client, server, user agent, origin, intermediary, gateway, proxy, and cache roles per exchange. Demonstrate the result through TypeScript 7 strict types plus WHATWG Fetch Request, Response, Headers, URL, streams, and AbortSignal contracts.

What must be established before implementation?

Evidence target: Fetch and TypeScript Commit to the mission model · Workshop Client Server Roles.
steps.why: The decision supplies observable evidence for distinguish client, server, user agent, origin, intermediary, gateway, proxy, and cache roles per exchange. demonstrate the result through typescript 7 strict types plus whatwg fetch request, response, headers, url, streams, and abortsignal contracts. For Fetch and TypeScript Commit to the mission model · Workshop Client Server Roles, keep Client Server Roles connected to executable HTTP client decision evidence.

options.text: For test an acceptance boundary, run one convenient Layered Failure Evidence happy path, omit method, authority, credential, byte, time, redirect, retry, and cleanup boundaries, then accept plausible output.
Reject the unsupported conclusion, name the violated Layered Failure Evidence condition, repair the responsible layer, and rerun the boundary trace.
Keep the plausible output and hide the assumption to avoid confusing the stakeholder. That claim does not survive the test an acceptance boundary evidence boundary.

content.title: Evidence defense
content.text: Use the case, one assumption, one observable result, and one limitation. Do not copy the competency statement: Classify name resolution, connection, TLS, protocol, status, representation, and business failures without collapsing them.
